title: The ‘MCC Effect’ is real. The Trump administration must not overlook it.
gist: Former US ambassador Richard K. Bell argues the Millennium Challenge Corporation's scorecard model — which requires countries to beat half their peers on 22 third-party-sourced governance indicators, not just clear an absolute bar — genuinely drove Côte d'Ivoire's governance and investment-climate improvements from 2011 onward, and that the Trump administration's foreign-assistance review risks discarding a cheap, effective diplomatic lever.

insights.claim: MCC compact eligibility requires passing an annual scorecard now measuring 22 governance indicators (up from 20), with data sourced from independent third parties rather than from the recipient government or from US bilateral relations.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: The third-party sourcing is what makes the mechanism hard to game diplomatically — a structural feature that differentiates MCC from politically-negotiated aid and makes its incentive effects more credible.
insights.quote: it’s not enough for a regime to have good bilateral relations with Washington
insights.locator: The information that is used to determine each indicator

insights.claim: To pass an MCC scorecard indicator, a country must clear an absolute score threshold and also outperform at least half of its comparator countries, so a country can improve its own absolute score year-over-year and still fail an indicator if peers improved faster.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: A relative, moving-target design forces continuous reform effort rather than a one-time fix — relevant for modeling how durable MCC-driven governance gains are likely to be.
insights.quote: it must also do better than half of its comparator countries
insights.locator: And a country can never rest on its laurels

insights.claim: Securing an MCC compact after passing the scorecard requires a country to negotiate a tailored proposal, with grants that can total hundreds of millions of dollars disbursed over roughly five years.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: Sizes the financial incentive countries are chasing when they undertake MCC-linked governance reforms.
insights.quote: Such grants can amount to hundreds of millions of dollars over five years

insights.claim: President Alassane Ouattara made passing the MCC scorecard a government priority starting when he took office in 2011, and Côte d'Ivoire achieved it within a few years, eventually securing a compact whose budget ceiling rose to more than $536 million over six years.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of the scorecard's incentive effect translating into a nine-figure aid commitment when a government treats it as a governance target.
insights.quote: a compact whose budget ceiling eventually rose to more than $536 million over six years
insights.locator: It did so within just a few years

insights.claim: Côte d'Ivoire graduated from lower-income to lower-middle-income status during Bell's tenure as ambassador, which moved it into a more demanding comparator group and caused it to briefly fail the scorecard before it rose to the tougher standard.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: Shows the scorecard's peer-relative design in action: economic success itself raises the governance bar, illustrating the mechanism is self-adjusting rather than a fixed hurdle.
insights.quote: it was no surprise that in its first year in that group, it did not pass the scorecard

insights.claim: Côte d'Ivoire's ranking in Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index rose from 130th in 2012 to 105th in 2021, then to 99th by the end of Bell's tenure in 2023, and to 76th most recently.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: An external, third-party corruption metric tracking a sustained multi-year improvement that the author attributes partly to sustained MCC-linked pressure — a concrete governance-trend data point for country-risk models on Côte d'Ivoire.
insights.quote: Côte d’Ivoire had risen to seventy-sixth
insights.locator: And less than two years later

insights.claim: Despite the Trump administration's scaling back of the MCC, Côte d'Ivoire has secured another compact with potential funding of up to $300 million.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: Shows the MCC pipeline is still delivering nine-figure commitments even amid the agency's uncertain political future — a data point against assuming the program is already defunct.
insights.quote: Côte d’Ivoire has secured another compact with potential funding of up to $300 million
insights.locator: And now, despite the Trump administration’s scaling back

insights.claim: Bell argues the Trump administration and its successors should continue using the MCC globally given its proven effectiveness, implicitly warning against the foreign-assistance review's threat to the agency's future.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: The article's core policy ask — a signal that MCC-linked development and investment-climate improvements in eligible countries could stall or reverse if the agency is further cut.
insights.quote: The Trump administration and its successors should continue to make good use of the MCC globally, given its proven effectiveness as a valuable tool of foreign policy.
insights.locator: The Trump administration and its successors

insights.claim: Whether or not a country ultimately secures a compact, publicly passing the MCC scorecard alone makes a country more attractive to investors, since results are publicly available and serve as an independent due-diligence signal.
insights.speaker: Richard K. Bell
insights.so_what: Positions MCC scorecard status as a usable, low-cost proxy signal for investors screening frontier and emerging-market sovereign risk, independent of whether a compact is ultimately awarded.
insights.quote: Scorecard results are publicly available and are a handy resource for potential investors.
insights.locator: In my interactions with successive prime ministers
